primary_diagnosis.name: Symptomatic peripheral arterial disease (intermittent claudication) due to severe hyperlipidaemia/systemic atherosclerosis; limb currently viable

pathophysiology_summary: Intermittent claudication is exertional muscle ischaemia from flow-limiting atherosclerotic stenosis of the lower-limb arteries. Severe, chronic hyperlipidaemia (here betrayed by tendon xanthomas, xanthelasma and corneal arcus) drives systemic atherosclerosis; narrowed arteries deliver adequate flow at rest but cannot meet the increased demand of walking, so the calf muscles become ischaemic and cramp after a reproducible distance, resolving within minutes of rest as demand falls. Absent dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses with weak femoral pulses and bilateral carotid bruits indicate widespread disease. The key clinical distinction is between stable claudication (a viable limb, exertional pain, safe for urgent outpatient work-up) and acute or critical limb ischaemia (rest pain, tissue loss/ulceration, or the 6 Ps of acute occlusion - pain, pallor, pulselessness, paraesthesia, paralysis, poikilothermia) which is a surgical emergency; here the limb is viable at rest with no rest pain, ulceration or acute features. Management of stable PAD is antiplatelet therapy, high-intensity statin, aggressive risk-factor modification (smoking, diet, glycaemia, blood pressure) and a supervised/structured exercise (walk-through-pain) programme to promote collateral formation, with ankle-brachial index testing and vascular review. Because pulses and ABI cannot be assessed over telehealth and the symptoms have acutely worsened, prompt in-person vascular assessment is required, with clear safety-netting for limb-threatening features. The same atherosclerosis confers very high cardiovascular (MI/stroke) risk.

differential.diagnosis: Peripheral arterial disease with intermittent claudication
differential.evidence_basis: Reproducible exertional calf cramp relieved by rest is classic claudication.
Acute worsening over a week mandates active exclusion of a limb-threatening process, though absence of rest pain/6 Ps makes it currently unlikely.
Considered for calf pain, but bilateral exertional pain without swelling/erythema argues against DVT.
Can mimic vascular claudication, but relief by standing rest rather than spinal flexion, with absent pulses/bruits, favours a vascular cause.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The AI Doctor should recognise claudication while actively excluding acute/critical limb ischaemia and DVT, and must not over-triage a viable limb to emergency nor under-triage a possible limb threat.

differential.diagnosis: Stable symptomatic peripheral arterial disease (viable limb) from severe hyperlipidaemia
differential.evidence_basis: Reproducible claudication (DI-001) with no rest pain/ulceration/6 Ps (DI-003) and no DVT features (DI-004) indicates stable PAD in a viable limb, needing urgent outpatient work-up not emergency surgery.
Remains the key thing to exclude and safety-net given the acute worsening and inability to examine pulses remotely.
Bilateral exertional pain without swelling or erythema is not DVT.
Relief by standing rest and the vascular signs point away from a neurogenic cause.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After the red-flag screen this is stable PAD in a viable limb: urgent vascular assessment (ABI/pulses), antiplatelet + statin + risk-factor modification and structured exercise - with clear escalation instructions for limb-threatening features.

differential.diagnosis: Stable symptomatic peripheral arterial disease from severe hyperlipidaemia - presumptive, pending ABI, pulse examination and fasting lipids
differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis for telehealth. Pulses/ABI cannot be assessed remotely; prompt in-person vascular assessment confirms viability and severity, and lipids guide statin therapy.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final telehealth framing is stable PAD needing prompt (not emergency) vascular assessment and cardiovascular risk treatment, with explicit ED safety-netting for acute limb ischaemia.

red_flags.description: Reproducible intermittent claudication (exertional calf cramp relieved by rest)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Marks flow-limiting peripheral arterial disease and very high systemic cardiovascular risk requiring vascular work-up and risk-factor treatment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: PAD and its cardiovascular risk go untreated, with progression and elevated MI/stroke risk.

red_flags.description: Acute worsening of claudication over one week
red_flags.clinical_significance: Rapid deterioration raises concern for disease progression toward critical limb ischaemia and warrants prompt (not deferred) vascular assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A progressing limb threat could be missed if treated as unchanged chronic disease.

red_flags.description: Acute/critical limb ischaemia features (rest pain, ulceration, or a pale/cold/numb/paralysed foot - the 6 Ps)
red_flags.clinical_significance: These would indicate a limb-threatening emergency requiring immediate ED/vascular surgery; their absence supports safe urgent outpatient management, but they must be safety-netted.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: If these develop and are not acted on urgently, the limb can be lost.

red_flags.description: Bilateral carotid bruits, weak femoral pulses and absent dorsalis pedis/posterior tibial pulses on clinician examination
red_flags.clinical_significance: These examination signs confirm widespread atherosclerosis and PAD severity; they are clinician-elicited and not obtainable over telehealth.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not applicable to telehealth - requires in-person examination/ABI; the correct action is prompt vascular assessment.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Over-triaging a viable limb to the emergency department
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Anxiety about a clot could prompt an unnecessary ED visit; a viable limb with exertional pain relieved by rest is better served by urgent outpatient vascular assessment - while still safety-netting acute limb ischaemia.
The acute worsening and inability to examine pulses remotely mean the AI Doctor must actively screen for and safety-net rest pain and the 6 Ps rather than assume stability.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Treating it as just a leg problem
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Missing the systemic implication - absent pulses, carotid bruits and lipid stigmata mean very high MI/stroke risk requiring antiplatelet, statin and risk-factor treatment.
Rest relieves the pain but is not therapy; structured exercise (walk-through-pain), not rest, promotes collaterals, alongside medical therapy.

risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: Anticipated reduced ABI (<0.9) consistent with peripheral arterial disease
risk_scores.clinical_implication: A reduced ABI confirms PAD severity and guides vascular management; cannot be measured over telehealth and requires in-person testing.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Palpation of femoral, dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses
Direct assessment of limb temperature, colour and capillary refill for viability
Fasting lipid profile, HbA1c and 12-lead ECG
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Prompt in-person vascular assessment (urgent same-day to within 24-48 hours) for pulse examination and ABI, start an antiplatelet and arrange high-intensity statin after lipids, institute risk-factor modification and a structured walking programme, and refer for vascular review - with IMMEDIATE ED attendance if the leg develops constant rest pain or the foot turns pale, cold or numb (acute limb ischaemia).
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Either over-triaging a viable limb to an emergency, or under-triaging by reassuring without arranging pulse/ABI assessment and risk-factor treatment and without safety-netting acute limb ischaemia, would be errors.
